original_abstract: GROMACS has established itself as a premier engine for biomolecular simulation, evolving over two decades from a tool centered on single-core efficiency to a highly sophisticated framework capable of utilizing the world’s largest supercomputers. This paper explores the architectural and algorithmic transformations required to navigate the challenges of the exascale era. We detail the implementation of multi-level parallelism, which integrates wide SIMD acceleration, high-performance GPU offloading, and a hybrid MPI/OpenMP execution model. These advancements necessitated a fundamental redesign of molecular dynamics kernels—transitioning to a cluster-based neighbor-searching algorithm that maximizes throughput on both modern CPUs and accelerators. Beyond intra-node performance, we address strong-scaling bottlenecks through task decomposition and the management of long-range interactions, while highlighting the role of ensemble simulation frameworks in saturating massive computational resources. Finally, we discuss the evolution of the GROMACS development process, including the transition to C++, the integration of rigorous peer review, and continuous testing protocols essential for maintaining a robust, performance-oriented open-source codebase for the scientific community.
humanized_abstract: First published: 04 October 2017 Since its inception about two decades ago, GROMACS has developed into one of the most popular simulation software packages for biomolecular simulations. Starting from an era where a single core was the common unit of computer processing power to nowadays where the world’s fastest supercomputers consist of hundreds of thousands of such units is a formidable development. In this article we describe the many architectural and algorithmic changes that needed to be implemented for a simulation program to be capable of making efficient use of these resources. This involves turning GROMACS from a single-core performance optimised program into a multi-level parallel program that in principle fully utilises resources provided by wide SIMD operations, GPU acceleration and hybrid (MPI/OpenMP) multi-core processing, for which we also had to replace the present molecular dynamics integrator with a cluster algorithm that allows neighbours of particles to be located both in the processor and in accelerated memory. In addition we had to distribute tasks to fully remove intra-node strong scaling performance barriers, to implement schemes to handle slowly evolving long-range components, and to use techniques from ensemble simulation to fully exploit the massive resources now available. We highlight all these developments in the simulations of a 1.2 μm long single-c strand DNA polymer chain. We also give an overview of the changes in our development process that were also required to manage our open-source code to obtain a high performance simulation program such as that described in the rest of this paper. These include moving GROMACS to C++, implementation of rigorous code reviewing in the coding workflow and the implementation of full automatic testing of all parts of the codebase.
